coredata.dc:title: Measurement system for in-situ estimation of instantaneous corrosion rate in supercritical water

coredata.dc:creator: Valtr, Jaromír
Roztočil, Petr
Dašek, David
Mušálek, Radek
Lukáč, František

Mištová, Eva
Jelínek, Luděk
Sajdl, Petr
Macák, Jan
coredata.dc:description: Current research of cladding and in-core materials for the supercritical water reactor concept is focused on Fe-Cr-Ni alloys (e.g. 800 H, 310 S). Along the standard measurements of general corrosion rate of materials by exposure testing in autoclaves, where poor reproducibility is an issue, especially when comparing results from different experimental facilities, a good alternative may be the use of in-situ electrochemical measurements. The use of electrochemical methods is, however, more challenging due to complicated instrumentation as well as due to the supercritical water conditions. This paper is focused on the design of an experimental facility for in-situ electrochemical testing at supercritical water conditions, i.e. main circulating loop with autoclave, including the design of the electrode system, electrode leads and their surface insulation coating. Stability of electrochemical measurements has been tested using 2- and 3-electrode set-ups. Finally, surface insulation coating of the electrode leads with the best chemical and mechanical stability during experiment has been chosen for the presented application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
Nuclear industry
Nickel alloys
Plasmasprayed, insulation
High temperature corrosion
Supercritical water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimation of impedance of electrode leads insulation
Testing of stability of ceramic shielding tubes
Estimation of impedance of electrode leads insulation
In-situ electrochemical impedance measurements in supercritical water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Autoclave and test loop: ES – electrode system; T – thermocouple; P – pressure gauge; V1, V2 – service valves; SV1, SV2 – safety valves; RV – back pressure regulator; MV1 – mixing chamber, 3wV6 and 3wV7 – sampling valves, 3wV1 to 3wV5 – analytical loop service 3-way valves.
Cross-section of as-sprayed coatings. Overview. SEM-BSE.
Cross-section of as-sprayed coatings. Detail. SEM-BSE.
Cross-section of coatings after exposure. Detail. SEM-BSE.
Bode diagram of impedance response of the AISI 347 lead inserted in Degussit Al23 shield tube and AISI 347 covered by plasma sprayed YSZ coating. Measured at 25 °C, 0.5 M K2SO4.
Complex plane plot of electrochemical impedance spectrum of 800 H alloy in supercritical water (380 °C, 25 MPa) Spectra measured in 2-electrode setup (a.) and 3-electrode set-up (b.).
Complex plane plot of electrochemical impedance spectrum of autoclave in supercritical water (380 °C, 25 MPa).
Complex plane plot of electrochemical impedance spectra of 316 L and 310 S steel in supercritical water (500 °C, 25 MPa) after long-term exposure.
Polarization curves of AISI 310 S steel in supercritical water at 380 °C and 25 MPa. Data presented as measured and after post-run compensation of iR drop.
Phase composition of coatings according to XRD.
Dissolution rate of different types of ceramics in supercritical water at 25 MPa and 380 °C.
Impedance parameters of various types of lead insulation. R – resistance across the insulation, C dl – Helmholtz capacitance, C pore – capacitance in pores of the coating.
Equivalent circuit parameters of electrode responses in 2- and 3-electrode set-ups. Parameters are not normalized to the electrode surface area.
Comparison of faradaic impedance parameters of electrode responses in 2- and 3-electrode set-ups and the corresponding corrosion current density. Normalized to the electrode surface area.

Impedance parameters of 316 L and 310 S electrodes exposed in SCW at 500 °C/25 MPa. Electric insulation of 316 L electrode leads was performed by zirconia shielding tubes, while in case of 310 S plasma sprayed zirconia coating was used.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *RSE (R ct ) – relative standard error of R ct estimation by data fitting

head.ce:title: Measurement system for in-situ estimation of instantaneous corrosion rate in supercritical water

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Plasma Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences, Za Slovankou 3, Prague 8, Czech Republic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Current research of cladding and in-core materials for the supercritical water reactor concept is focused on Fe-Cr-Ni alloys (e.g. 800 H, 310 S). Along the standard measurements of general corrosion rate of materials by exposure testing in autoclaves, where poor reproducibility is an issue, especially when comparing results from different experimental facilities, a good alternative may be the use of in-situ electrochemical measurements. The use of electrochemical methods is, however, more challenging due to complicated instrumentation as well as due to the supercritical water conditions. This paper is focused on the design of an experimental facility for in-situ electrochemical testing at supercritical water conditions, i.e. main circulating loop with autoclave, including the design of the electrode system, electrode leads and their surface insulation coating. Stability of electrochemical measurements has been tested using 2- and 3-electrode set-ups. Finally, surface insulation coating of the electrode leads with the best chemical and mechanical stability during experiment has been chosen for the presented application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of supercritical water in nuclear reactors has been considered for more than half a century. Supercritical water is currently used as cooling medium in commercial non-nuclear power industry in more than 400 units worldwide. Although much of the operating experience as well as the use of constructional materials in fossil fired power plants and conventional pressurized and boiling light water reactors may be employed for the design of many components of the steam cycle, the design of the reactor core and the constructional materials used there must obviously be entirely new [1,2].

The most challenging problem are materials suitable for the fuel cladding, as they have to withstand much higher temperatures than structural materials in other parts of the core. Initially, available materials only allowed a maximum peak cladding temperature up to 550 °C. Ti-stabilized 15Cr 15Ni alloy 1.4970 was preselected for the European concept HPLWR as a suitable candidate for fuel cladding [1], and later the ODS (oxide dispersion strengthened) ferritic-martensitic steel PM2000, however, this choice was finally dismissed as well due to possible anisotropy of its mechanical properties [3]. However, thermohydraulic analyses for the HPLWR showed that the peak cladding temperature will reach up to 630 °C [4]. Therefore, the current candidates are austenitic stainless steels, such as steel 310S in various modifications (standard, Zr-modified, low carbon) [5] and Ni-based alloys, such as the alloys of the Inconel and Incoloy series [6,7]. Currently, properties of 800 H, 310 S and 310 S AFA are examined in the framework of the ECC-SMART (Joint European Canadian Chinese development of Small Modular Reactor Technology) project [8].

Exposure tests are the most common type of tests to evaluate general corrosion resistance of materials, however, they suffer from poor reproducibility, especially when comparing results from different laboratories. As reported in [9,10], results of exposure tests performed within two interlaboratory comparison exercises (also called round-robin tests) showed very large scatter between data obtained in different laboratories (i.e. different test facilities). Initial trend of results depending on flow rate through the autoclave obtained in the first round-robin test was reversed in the second one, hence the reason behind the observed differences remains unclear. On the other hand, in-situ electrochemical measurements of corrosion rate may offer several advantages over traditional exposure tests. These advantages include operation free of repeated temperature and pressure trips due to repeated start-ups and shut-downs or corrosion rate data unaffected by possible spallation of the oxide or deposition of corrosion products on specimens. However, the dependence of results on flow rate through the facility may still be expected even for electrochemical measurements.

Furthermore, given that the time to measure one impedance spectrum is around 24–36 h (depending on the final frequency), each measurement providing one value of instantaneous corrosion rate, it is technically possible to obtain many measurement points along the experiment, which is desirable to understand the corrosion kinetics. Integral corrosion rate may then be calculated over the entire duration of the experiment (1000 h), which may be compared to data available in literature. A major disadvantage of electrochemical in-situ methods is very complex instrumentation and complexity of the exposure equipment with respect to the high operating parameters as well as the need to carefully design and insulate the electrode system.

This paper presents the first results of measurements of corrosion rate using in-situ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy in supercritical water. Care has been given to properly design the electrode system and evaluate the response of different electrode system set-ups. Attention has been paid to properly insulate the electrical leads (wires) to the working electrodes (measured samples), which are relatively long with a large surface area, hence it was presumed that their response could contribute to the electrode impedance response. Several insulating materials have been tested with the aim to select a ceramic, plasma-sprayed coating for the lead made of stainless steel with the highest mechanical and chemical stability. Impedance spectra have been acquired in both 2- and 3-electrode set-ups and equivalent circuits have been proposed for evaluation. At the end of the experiment, polarization curves have been measured, which provided the Tafel slopes for the estimation of the integral corrosion rate.

Deposition of the coatings was carried out using hybrid water/argon stabilized plasma spraying system WSP-H 500 (ProjectSoft HK a.s., Czech Republic) operated at 500 A (∼160 kW). Delivered wires were grit-blasted by alumina grit prior to the deposition of the coatings. Three different coatings chemistries were deposited - ZrSiO4, YSZ (8 % Yttria Stabilized Zirconia), and Al2O3. Spraying parameters are listed in Table 1. Rods were mounted on a rotating holder and preheated by plasma torch. Then, the powder injection into the plasma torch was started and coating was applied in several consecutive deposition passes until the desired coating thickness was deposited. Despite a very small diameter of the wires (2 mm) and their low stiffness, coatings without any apparent cracking or delaminations were successfully deposited.

To inspect the coatings microstructure before and after SCW exposition, cross-sections were prepared. Samples were cut and cold-mounted into epoxy under vacuum and polished using semi-automatic polishing system Tegramin-25 (Struers, Denmark). Samples were observed by Apreo 2 S LoVac (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Czech Republic) scanning electron microscope (SEM) in compositional back-scattered electrons (BSE) imaging mode. Phase composition was determined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) by D8 Discover diffractometer (Bruker AXS, Germany) in symmetrical geometry using Cu anode and Rietveld refinement method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of impedance of electrode leads insulation
ce:section.ce:para: 2 mm stainless steel wires (AISI 347) covered by plasma sprayed ceramic coatings described above were used for electrode leads. Alternatively, electric insulation was realized by inserting the leads into tubes made of alumina (Degussit Al23, Kyocera). The leads were exposed to SCW environment during autoclave tests, therefore, impedance of the insulated leads was estimated. Furthermore, impedance of leads insulated with ceramic plasma sprayed coatings was compared to insulation realized by insertion of the leads into alumina tubes mentioned above, a simpler alternative to plasma spraying. The alumina tubes used in this experiment had outer diameter 2.8 mm, inner diameter 2.2 mm and length 100 mm. Typically 4 pieces of tubes were used to insulate the entire length of the lead. Impedance of the insulated leads was tested at laboratory temperature in a set-up consisting of the insulated lead (as working electrode, WE), Pt wire as pseudo-reference electrode and stainless steel tube as counter electrode (CE). The lead was inserted in the CE tube with both ends insulated by silicone sealing, so that only the plasma covered surface of the lead was exposed to the electrolyte. The length of the lead and the stainless steel tube was 40 cm. The reference electrode was placed in the space between WE and CE. The space was filled with 0.5 M K2SO4 solution in demineralized water, serving as electrolyte. Impedance spectra were measured in the range starting 100 kHz to 1 mHz in potentiostatic mode at open circuit potential (OCP).

The electrode system contained two working electrodes ( Fig. 1) in order to verify reproducibility of the results. The working electrodes were identical full rods of Alloy 800 H or AISI 310 S stainless steel with diameter of 10 mm and length of 50 mm. The leads were connected to the working electrodes using M2 threads. A platinum wire spiral was placed symmetrically between the working electrodes to ensure as identical set-ups as possible for both working electrodes. The platinum wire served as pseudo-reference electrode because of the technical difficulties with construction of reference electrodes for high temperature environments as well as their installation into a high-temperature, high-pressure system [11].

At the beginning of the experimental sequence the autoclave system was pressurized to 25 ± 1 MPa and heated to the nominal temperature of 380 or 500 ± 2 °C with applied thermal gradient 1–3 °C∙min−1. After reaching nominal conditions, constant conditions were maintained for the duration of the entire experiment (usually 1000 h). The environment of the experiment was either deoxygenated (< 5 ppb) demineralized water, or low-oxygen supercritical water (c(O2) = 150 ppb). Flowrate of the medium through the autoclave was 300 ml∙h−1 which corresponds to 1.6 autoclave volumes per hour.

Potentiostatic EIS measurements were performed regularly on both working electrodes with a frequency of measurement less than 24 h at the beginning of the experiment up to 168 h at the end of the experiment, hence the time evolution of the characteristics of the studied samples could be obtained. Impedance spectra were measured at the corrosion potential, with potential perturbation having amplitude of 15 mV, in the frequency range starting at 1 MHz, the lower frequency limit was set as low as possible, at higher exposure times reaching between 0.05 and 0.1 mHz. The low frequency limit depended on the stability of the individual measurements and on the influence of measurement time elapsed, which clearly influenced the final data points. Data, which were influenced by the long measuring times were discarded from evaluation.

Due to the EIS measurement stability testing, 2- and 3- electrode setup was used. In a previous study [12], it has been shown that the response of the system may differ depending on the electrode system set-up, as it influences the appearance of parasitic elements in the spectrum as well as the stability of the impedance measurements, especially at low frequencies. In the 2-electrode set-up, the autoclave body was used as both the reference and counter electrode, in the 3-electrode set-up, the Pt wire was used as the pseudo-reference electrode and the autoclave body served as the counter electrode.

Overall view and details of the as-sprayed wires cross-sections (
Figs. 2 and 3) show good uniformity of the coating thickness and porosity distribution. Despite an extremely small radius of curvature of the sprayed substrates, all coatings developed standard lamellar microstructure consisting of well-flattened splats. Also, porosity and phase composition of the coatings was comparable to the coatings conventionally sprayed on flat substrates ( Table 2). Detailed view of the ZrSiO4 and Al2O3 coatings showed some delamination of the coating from the substrate. However, these delamination cracks presumably developed during the cutting procedure due to vibrations imposed on highly flexible rods and limited strain tolerance of the coatings.

According to XRD (Table 2), as-sprayed ZrSiO4 coating consisted of tetragonal and monoclinic ZrO2 in approx. ratio 3:1. However, formation of amorphous silica, which may not be detected by XRD, is also expected in the coating [13]. Some silica may be also lost due to the volatilization in hot plasma. After the exposure, the ratio of tetragonal to monoclinic ZrO2 phase did not change significantly, but increase in the coating porosity was observed which indicates leaching of silica from the deposit. This was also in agreement with post-run analysis of the autoclave content. It was found that the concentration of Si in the autoclave environment could be as high as units of mg∙dm−3 after 1000 h long experiment.
YSZ coatings did not show any major changes in the coating’s microstructure during SCW exposure, declaring excellent stability of the coating in the SCW environment.

The greatest changes were observed for Al2O3 coating. As-sprayed coating contained about 10 % of corundum (α-alumina) phase and 90 % of metastable γ/δ-alumina phase resulting from the rapid solidification of molten droplets after their impact onto the substrate [14]. After the SCW exposure, increased density of intrasplat cracks was observed near the coating surface, but more importantly, a new phase was apparent on the cross-sections in the compositional BSE mode (darker spots deep inside the coating in Fig. 5). This phase was not accessible by surface XRD scan, so some of the coating was delaminated, crushed, homogenized and analyzed by XRD again. The results showed that this newly formed phase was Boehmite AlO(OH), which presumably formed due hydration of the initial coating upon exposure to high-temperature, high-pressure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of stability of ceramic shielding tubes
ce:section.ce:para: During the experiments in SCW, ceramic shielding tubes and beads were used for electric insulation. Supercritical water can be very aggressive to certain types of inorganic materials. The long-term test was performed with the aim to estimate stability of potential ceramic materials in supercritical water. As the sample tubes made of YSZ (yttria stabilized zirconia - Degussit FZY, Kyocera), alumina (Degussit Al23, Kyocera) and MSZ (magnesia stabilized zirconia - FZM, Kyocera) were tested. The samples were exposed in autoclave to supercritical water at pressure of 25 MPa and at temperature of 380 °C for 500 h. No change in surface roughness and no cracks or voids were evidenced on any of the samples after the exposure. Weight losses were determined and dissolution rates of the ceramics in mg∙h−1∙m−2 were calculated. The results are presented in Table 3.

MSZ samples showed negligible dissolution rate in SCW while measurable weight loss was obtained with YSZ and alumina. Nevertheless, area of the ceramic insulation exposed during the autoclave tests to supercritical water was in the order of units of cm2, so the rate of contamination of the loop environment is expected to not exceed low units of micrograms per hour even in case of YSZ or alumina.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of impedance of electrode leads insulation
ce:section.ce:para:
Preliminary impedance loss estimation of the leads was performed during ex-situ tests at laboratory temperature in 0.5 M K2SO4 solution. Approximately 2–3 h were necessary to stabilize the electrode potential. Amplitude of the sine potential perturbation was 10–15 mV. Impedance data (Fig. 5) were analysed and resistance and capacitance were estimated (
Table 4). All the leads with plasma sprayed coating had very similar responses, so only YSZ covered lead response is presented in Fig. 5. The resistance of the coatings was also very similar in all cases. Capacitance analysis showed somewhat higher values for the silicate-based coating, indicating higher porosity compared to YSZ or Al2O3 coating. The most complicated impedance response was that of 347 stainless steel lead inserted in Degussit tubing. The impedance included the response of the lead’s surface, intermediate water layer and the ceramic tubing. In this case, the overall impedance was approximately 1 order of magnitude lower than that of the plasma sprayed coatings. Together with the 3 types of coatings described in 3.1.1 section, olivine (MgFe)2SiO4 coating was tested as well. This coating had unsatisfactory properties compared to the others and was therefore excluded from further autoclave applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ electrochemical impedance measurements in supercritical water
ce:section.ce:para: Impedance spectra on both working electrodes were measured in 2- and 3-electrode set-ups. Both types of spectra measured on the same working electrode are shown for comparison in Fig. 6.

The spectrum measured in the 3-electrode set-up contains two time constants comprised of parallel combinations of a resistance and a constant phase element (CPE). The high-frequency time constant represents the response of the environment (demineralized water) and the low-frequency time constant is the response of the faradaic process (the corrosion reaction) proceeding at the interface of the working electrode (WE). The corrosion reaction is represented by the charge transfer resistance R ct and the double-layer capacitance C dl; the charge transfer resistance being inversely proportional to the corrosion rate. Therefore, the spectrum was evaluated using the two-time-constant equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 8.

On the other hand, impedance spectrum measured on the same working electrode in the 2-electrode set-up contained an additional time constant in medium frequencies, as compared to the 3-electrode set-up. In the 2-electrode set-up, where the body of the autoclave is connected simultaneously as both the reference and the counter electrode, the measured impedance spectrum contains also the response of the autoclave itself. The spectrum of the autoclave, measured in 3-electrode set-up with the Pt-wire pseudo-reference electrode and one of the working electrodes serving as the counter electrode, is shown in
Fig. 7. The low-frequency R-CPE combination is the one that appears in the impedance spectrum measured on the Alloy 800 H sample when the 2-electrode set-up is applied. Hence, all spectra measured in 2-electrode set-up have been evaluated using the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 9, which contained one additional R-CPE combination, i.e. a total of three R-CPE combinations.

However, comparison of the low-frequency data of the working electrode measured in 2- and 3-electrode mode indicates that the presence of the autoclave response does not have any significant influence on the evaluation of the charge transfer resistance whether measured in 2- or 3-electrode set-up.

In order to provide an estimation of the corrosion rate, the charge transfer resistance was calculated using the Stern-Geary equation and assuming that the sample is in passive state (β a → ∞) and estimating the cathodic Tafel slope to β C = 0,15 mV.

The impedance data obtained in the measurement system described above were compared with those measured on steel 316 L in a previous work [12]. In
Fig. 10, impedance data measured at the same supercritical water parameters with 316 L and 310 S steel electrodes after long-term exposure are compared. The lead to 316 L and 310 S electrodes were insulated by zirconia tubing and by plasma sprayed zirconia, respectively.

The shape of the 316 L and 310 S spectra in complex plane is similar showing two time constants with water impedance response at high frequencies and charge transfer – double layer response at low frequencies. Results of spectra fitting are presented in Table 7showing that overall similar values were obtained, even in completely different measurement systems. Regarding the corrosion rate parameters, a higher value of charge transfer resistance in case of 310 S steel is apparent, what could be ascribed to an expected lower oxidation rate of 310 S steel in SCW due to a higher Ni and Cr content compared to 316 L [12]. Another noticeable difference is the relatively lower impedance data scatter of 310 S data in the entire frequency range. It can be ascribed to the positive effect of plasma sprayed insulation on the suppression of parasitic signals generated from leads. A lower scatter of impedance data has an impact on a lower standard error of charge transfer resistance and relatively better overall quality of fit characterised by a lower χ2 value. Also, lower noise at low frequencies enables extending of impedance measurement to sub-milihertz range.

Characterisation of anodic and cathodic processes at the electrode interface can be performed by measurement of polarization curves. This technique was already extensively used in high temperature environment, e.g. for corrosion studies in light water reactor coolant environment [15]. Polarisation curves describe the relationship between electrode potential and current. From this relationship, the type of reaction control can be determined. Moreover, Tafel constants can be estimated from the linear parts of polarization curves. Tafel constants are important kinetic parameters, which are used to calculate the corrosion current from polarization resistance data [10]. Example of polarization curve measured with AISI 310S steel in supercritical water at 380 °C is shown in Fig. 10. Due to the very high resistivity of supercritical water, polarization data must be corrected for potential drop (iR drop) on the resistance R e between the working and reference electrodes. In Fig. 10, the raw polarization data and data after iR e drop post-run compensation are depicted. Value of resistance R e was estimated from high frequency part of the impedance spectrum measured before the polarization test.
Experimental testing facility for in-situ electrochemical testing of corrosion characteristics of candidate in-core materials for the SCWR has been designed and tested.

Special care was taken to design proper electrode system with well electrically insulated electrode leads. Several plasma-sprayed ceramic coatings have been tested and their performance evaluated. Partial phase change and delamination were evidenced in case of Al2O3 coating. The performance of shielding tubes made of YSZ, MSZ (magnesia-stabilized-zirconia) and Al2O3 in supercritical water was also tested. Extremely low dissolution rate was found in case of YSZ and alumina and dissolution beneath detection in case of MSZ. In ex-situ impedance measurements, it was found that plasma-sprayed coating can diminish impedance loss of the leads by one order of magnitude compared to simply using shielding tubes on working electrode leads.

Impedance response of the working electrodes has been evaluated by appropriate equivalent circuits depending on the electrode set-up. In 2-electrode set-up, the response of the autoclave body, which was used as counter electrode in all measurements, was found at medium frequencies of the working electrode’s response. In 3-electrode set-up, the impedance spectra did not contain this artefact.

Polarization curves have been measured at the end of the experiment (after 1000 h). Due to the low conductivity of the electrolyte, data must be compensated for the iR drop in order to properly estimate the Tafel slopes.
Results of experiments performed using the measurement system described in this paper will follow shortly. In these papers, results of 1000 h experiments on alloys 800H and 310S will be published.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the European Commission for financial support through the project ECC-SMART No. 945234. The work was also supported from the grant of Specific university research – grant No. A1_FTOP_2023_001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reference Electrodes for electrochemical measurements in high-temperature high-pressure aqueous environments—Review of potential corrections for ‘external’ reference systems
